title: Nepali Dal Bhat Tarkari

ingredients.name: toor dal (pigeon pea lentils)
visual_checks: Rinse thoroughly until water runs clear.
ingredients.notes: Rinse thoroughly until water runs clear.

subs.name: Masoor dal (red lentils) cooks faster (2 whistles) and produces an equally thick, comforting dal.

ingredients.name: rice, washed
visual_checks: Any medium or short-grain rice works; Nepali households use local rice varieties.
ingredients.notes: Any medium or short-grain rice works; Nepali households use local rice varieties.

subs.name: Cauliflower florets or cubed winter squash make an excellent seasonal tarkari substitute.
Fenugreek leaves (methi), mustard greens, or Swiss chard all work well in the tarkari.

ingredients.name: tomato, finely chopped
visual_checks: Used in both the dal and tarkari.
ingredients.notes: Used in both the dal and tarkari.

ingredients.name: onion, finely chopped
visual_checks: One for the dal tempering, one for the tarkari.
ingredients.notes: One for the dal tempering, one for the tarkari.

ingredients.name: lemon juice
visual_checks: Squeezed into the dal just before serving for brightness.
ingredients.notes: Squeezed into the dal just before serving for brightness.

steps.text: Pressure-cook the rinsed toor dal with ½ tsp turmeric, 2 cups water, and a pinch of salt for 3 whistles on medium flame. Open after the pressure releases naturally. The dal should be fully soft and porridge-like — mash it slightly with a ladle.

While the dal cooks, start the rice: rinse the rice and cook in a separate saucepan with 3 cups water and a pinch of salt. Bring to a boil, reduce heat to very low, cover, and cook for 15 minutes. Turn off heat and rest covered for 5 minutes — do not open the lid during cooking.

Make the dal tadka: heat 1.5 tbsp oil in a small frying pan. Add the cumin seeds and let them crackle. Add half the garlic and ginger, and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add one chopped tomato and half the onion; cook for 3 minutes until the tomato softens. Pour this tempering into the cooked dal and stir. Add lemon juice (if using) and adjust salt.

Make the tarkari: heat 1.5 tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat. Add the remaining onion and fry for 4 minutes until softened. Add the remaining garlic, ginger, red chilli powder, coriander powder, remaining turmeric, and a splash of water; stir for 1 minute.

Add the cubed potatoes and remaining chopped tomato to the tarkari. Stir to coat, add 3 tbsp water, cover, and cook on medium-low heat for 12 minutes until the potatoes are just tender. Add the spinach, stir until wilted — about 2 minutes. Sprinkle garam masala and adjust salt.

Plate the meal: a large serving of rice in the centre of the plate, dal poured alongside or over, and tarkari to the side. Serve with achar (pickle) and papad if available. Dal bhat is meant to be refilled generously from the pot — a second serving is expected and welcomed.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: toor-dal → Masoor dal (red lentils) cooks faster (2 whistles) and produces an equally thick, comforting dal.; potato → Cauliflower florets or cubed winter squash make an excellent seasonal tarkari substitute.; spinach → Fenugreek leaves (methi), mustard greens, or Swiss chard all work well in the tarkari.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This meal is almost entirely at home in an Indian kitchen — pressure cooker for dal, separate pot for rice, kadai for tarkari is a perfectly standard setup. The only distinguishing Nepali touch is timur (Szechuan pepper) in some versions, which can be approximated with extra black pepper and a small pinch of ground sichuan pepper from specialty stores. Serve with Indian-style mango or mixed pickle as the achar.
